blocks.cap: The box has \(30\) pieces, so the moment you call the squares \(s\), the triangles have nowhere to hide. They are everything left over, which is \(30 - s\). One letter now describes both groups. Each square brings \(4\) corners and each triangle brings \(3\), so the corners stack up as \(4s\) from the squares and \(3(30 - s)\) from the triangles. Setting that against the \(104\) corners in the box gives \(4s + 3(30 - s) = 104\), a single equation in a single unknown built straight from the picture.

blocks.x: Marisol keeps a slow, patient tortoise named Pebble in her backyard. She likes to think about how Pebble keeps growing older. She notices something neat about the numbers. Six years from now, Pebble will be exactly seven sixths of its current age. How old is Pebble right now, in years?
hints: Pick a letter for the thing you do not know yet, the age right now, and call it \(t\). A future age is just the current age plus however many years pass, so in six years Pebble will be \(t + 6\). The words say that future age equals seven sixths of the age now, so set \(t + 6\) equal to \(\tfrac{7}{6}t\).

Start from \(t + 6 = \tfrac{7}{6}t\). The fraction is annoying, so multiply every single term by \(6\) to clear it, which gives \(6t + 36 = 7t\). Now it is an ordinary equation with the variable on both sides, so subtract \(6t\) from each side and read off \(t\).
blocks.sol: Let \(t\) be Pebble's age right now, in years. That is the number the question is really after. Six years from now Pebble will be six years older, so the future age is \(t + 6\). The words tell us that future age is seven sixths of the current age, which means \(t + 6 = \tfrac{7}{6}t\). To get rid of the fraction, multiply every term by \(6\), giving \(6t + 36 = 7t\). Subtract \(6t\) from both sides and you are left with \(36 = t\), so \(t = 36\). The question asked for Pebble's age now, and that is exactly what \(t\) stands for. Check it against the words. In six years Pebble would be \(36 + 6 = 42\), and seven sixths of \(36\) is \(\tfrac{7}{6}\cdot 36 = 42\) as well, so the future age really does match. Pebble is \(\boxed{36}\) years old now.

blocks.x: You are not the first person to name an unknown and chase it through a sentence. Almost four thousand years ago in ancient Egypt, a scribe named Ahmes copied out a long scroll that we now call the Rhind Mathematical Papyrus, named after Alexander Henry Rhind, who bought it in the eighteen hundreds. Among its problems are puzzles the Egyptians called aha problems, where aha was simply their word for the unknown heap or quantity. A typical aha problem asks for a quantity such that the quantity plus a fraction of itself adds up to some stated total, which is exactly the kind of modeling you are doing right now. They had no letter \(x\) and no equals sign, so they wrote the whole thing out in words and worked it through by hand. People have been turning words into equations for a very, very long time, just with different handwriting.

A sculptor is welding three steel rings into a hanging mobile, and she wants the rings to grow in even steps so the piece looks balanced. She picks three consecutive even numbers for their weights in kilograms, meaning each ring is \(2\) kilograms heavier than the one before it. When she sets all three on the scale together, they read \(96\) kilograms. The whole point of an even spacing is that once you name the lightest ring, the next is \(2\) more and the heaviest is \(2\) more again, so the three weights are locked together by that one starting choice. She needs to know the weight of the heaviest ring, since that is the one the ceiling hook has to hold. What is the weight, in kilograms, of the heaviest ring? Type the single number.
hints: Let \(n\) stand for the weight of the lightest ring in kilograms. Because the rings step up by \(2\) each time, the middle ring is \(n + 2\) and the heaviest is \(n + 4\). The three weights add to \(96\), so write that sum as an equation in \(n\).

Your equation is \(n + (n + 2) + (n + 4) = 96\). Combine the like terms on the left to get \(3n + 6 = 96\), then subtract \(6\) and divide by \(3\) to find \(n\). Once you have \(n\), the question wants the heaviest ring, which is \(n + 4\), not \(n\) itself.
blocks.sol: Start by naming the unknown. Let \(n\) be the weight of the lightest ring in kilograms. Since the rings are consecutive even numbers, each one is \(2\) kilograms heavier than the last, so the middle ring weighs \(n + 2\) and the heaviest weighs \(n + 4\). The three weights together read \(96\) kilograms, which turns the words into an equation. $$n + (n + 2) + (n + 4) = 96$$ Combine the like terms on the left. The three copies of \(n\) make \(3n\), and the loose numbers \(2\) and \(4\) make \(6\), so the equation becomes $$3n + 6 = 96.$$ Subtract \(6\) from both sides to get \(3n = 90\), then divide both sides by \(3\) to find \(n = 30\). So the lightest ring is \(30\) kilograms, the middle ring is \(32\), and the heaviest is \(34\). Here is the trap worth naming. The variable \(n\) came out to \(30\), but the question never asked for the lightest ring. It asked for the heaviest, which is \(n + 4 = 34\). Check it against the words. The weights \(30\), \(32\), and \(34\) step up by \(2\) each time and add to \(30 + 32 + 34 = 96\), and \(34\) is indeed the largest of the three. The heaviest ring weighs \(\boxed{34}\) kilograms.

blocks.x: Here are the two things I figured out today. When integers run one after another, I do not need a fresh letter for each one, I anchor the first to \(n\) and write the next as \(n+1\), the one after that as \(n+2\), and so on, and if they are consecutive even or odd integers I just step by \(2\) instead, so \(n\), \(n+2\), \(n+4\). I add them, set the sum equal to the total I was given, solve for \(n\), and then, since the question almost always wants one particular member of the run, I add the right step to \(n\) to read off the exact integer it asked for. The second thing matters even more. Checking means putting my answer back into the original sentence in plain English, not just into the equation I wrote, because a wrong equation built from a misread will still be satisfied perfectly by its own answer, so only re-reading the words can ever catch the misread.

A baker is packing a charity fair. She fills exactly \(40\) gift boxes, and every single box is either a small box that holds \(6\) cupcakes or a large box that holds \(10\) cupcakes. When she is done, the boxes hold \(312\) cupcakes in all. How many large boxes did the baker fill?
hints: You have two unknowns but they are tied together. Let \(s\) be the number of small boxes. Since every box is small or large and there are \(40\) boxes in total, the number of large boxes must be \(40 - s\). Now count cupcakes, the small boxes contribute \(6s\) and the large boxes contribute \(10\) of them each.

Write the cupcake count as one equation, \(6s + 10(40 - s) = 312\). Distribute and combine like terms to get \(-4s + 400 = 312\). Solve for \(s\), then remember the question asks for large boxes, which is \(40 - s\), not \(s\) itself.
blocks.sol: Let \(s\) be the number of small boxes the baker fills. Every box is either small or large and there are \(40\) boxes altogether, so the two counts add to \(40\) and the number of large boxes is \(40 - s\). That single relationship is what lets one variable carry the whole problem. Now count cupcakes. The small boxes hold \(6\) each, so they account for \(6s\) cupcakes, and the large boxes hold \(10\) each, so they account for \(10(40 - s)\) cupcakes. The total is \(312\), which gives the equation $$6s + 10(40 - s) = 312.$$ Distribute the \(10\) to get \(6s + 400 - 10s = 312\), then combine the like terms \(6s\) and \(-10s\) to get \(-4s + 400 = 312\). Subtract \(400\) from both sides, so \(-4s = -88\), and divide by \(-4\) to find \(s = 22\). So there are \(22\) small boxes. The question did not ask for \(s\), it asked for the large boxes, which is \(40 - s = 40 - 22 = 18\). Check it against the words. With \(22\) small boxes and \(18\) large boxes the total is \(22 + 18 = 40\) boxes, and the cupcakes come to \(6 \cdot 22 + 10 \cdot 18 = 132 + 180 = 312\), both exactly right. The baker filled \(\boxed{18}\) large boxes.

review.x: A street magician is guessing the number written on a card you hold. She says, "Four more than triple your number lands exactly on thirty-one." What number is on your card?
hints: Let \(x\) be the number on the card. "Triple your number" is \(3x\), and "four more than" that adds 4, so the left side is \(3x + 4\).
Set \(3x + 4 = 31\). Subtract 4 from both sides, then divide by 3 to find \(x\).
review.sol: Let \(x\) be the number written on the card. Triple the number is \(3x\), and four more than that is \(3x + 4\). The clue says this lands on thirty-one, so the equation is $$3x + 4 = 31.$$ Subtract 4 from both sides to get \(3x = 27\), then divide both sides by 3 to get \(x = 9\). The question asks for the number on the card, which is \(\boxed{9}\). Check against the words. Triple of 9 is 27, and four more than 27 is 31, exactly as the magician said.

review.x: A lighthouse keeper logs how many ships pass each night. On Tuesday she counted some number of ships. On Wednesday a storm kept boats in the harbor, so the count was eight less than Tuesday's number, and that Wednesday count came out to twenty-three ships. How many ships passed on Tuesday?
hints: Let \(x\) be Tuesday's count. "Eight less than Tuesday's number" means you start at \(x\) and take 8 away, so it is \(x - 8\), not \(8 - x\). The order matters here.
You now have \(x - 8 = 23\). Add 8 to both sides to undo the subtraction and find \(x\).
review.sol: Let \(x\) be the number of ships that passed on Tuesday. The phrase "eight less than Tuesday's number" tells you to begin with Tuesday's count and remove 8, which is \(x - 8\). Watch the order, because "less than" flips it. It is \(x - 8\), never \(8 - x\). That Wednesday count equals 23, so the equation is $$x - 8 = 23.$$ Add 8 to both sides to get \(x = 23 + 8\), so \(x = 31\). The question asks for Tuesday's count, and that is exactly the \(x\) we solved for, so the answer is \(\boxed{31}\). Check it against the words. Eight less than 31 is \(31 - 8 = 23\), which matches the Wednesday count, so 31 is correct.

review.x: A museum tour starts the morning with fifty paper maps stacked at the front desk. By lunchtime a guide reaches for the stack and finds only twelve maps still sitting there, so some number of maps got handed out and taken off the pile during the morning. The handout count is the mystery here. Reading it as a sentence, that number of maps subtracted from the fifty leaves twelve behind. How many maps were handed out over the morning? Type the single number.
hints: Let \(x\) be the number of maps handed out. The maps came off the original fifty, so the fifty comes first and the handed-out amount is subtracted from it. That gives \(50 - x\), and what is left is \(12\). Be careful with the order, it is fifty minus the unknown, not the unknown minus fifty.

You now have \(50 - x = 12\). To get \(x\) by itself, you can add \(x\) to both sides and subtract \(12\) from both sides, which lines up as \(50 - 12 = x\). Finish that subtraction to find the number of maps.
review.sol: Let \(x\) stand for the number of maps handed out during the morning. The phrase that builds the equation is that number subtracted from the fifty, which means the fifty is the starting pile and \(x\) is taken away from it, written as \(50 - x\). What remains is twelve, so the equation is $$50 - x = 12.$$ Solve for \(x\). Add \(x\) to both sides to get \(50 = 12 + x\), then subtract \(12\) from both sides, which gives \(50 - 12 = x\), so \(x = 38\). The question asks how many maps were handed out, and that is exactly the number \(x\), so the answer is \(38\). Check it against the words. Thirty-eight maps subtracted from the original fifty leaves \(50 - 38 = 12\) maps still on the desk, which matches the twelve the guide found. So the number of maps handed out is \(\boxed{38}\).

review.x: A trail crew counts the number of new birdhouses they hung along a creek this spring. When you double that count and then add seven, you get thirty-three. How many birdhouses would be left if five of them were taken down?
hints: Let \(x\) be the number of birdhouses the crew hung. Doubling it gives \(2x\), and adding seven gives \(2x + 7\). That whole thing equals \(33\), so write \(2x + 7 = 33\).
Solve \(2x + 7 = 33\) to find \(x\). Then reread the question. It does not ask for \(x\) itself, it asks how many would be left if five were taken down, so compute \(x - 5\).
review.sol: Let \(x\) be the number of birdhouses the crew hung. Doubling the count gives \(2x\), and adding seven gives \(2x + 7\). The words say this equals thirty-three, so the equation is \(2x + 7 = 33\). Subtract \(7\) from both sides to get \(2x = 26\), then divide by \(2\) to get \(x = 13\). The crew hung \(13\) birdhouses. But the question asks how many would be left if five were taken down, which is \(13 - 5 = 8\). So the answer is \(\boxed{8}\). Check against the words. Double \(13\) to get \(26\), then add \(7\) to get \(33\), which matches "is thirty-three", and \(13 - 5 = 8\) leftover.

review.x: A small bakery splits its morning batch of croissants between two trays. The two trays hold 45 croissants together. The window tray holds 9 fewer croissants than the kitchen tray. How many croissants are on the kitchen tray?
hints: Let \(k\) stand for the number of croissants on the kitchen tray. The window tray holds 9 fewer, so write that as \(k - 9\).
The two trays together hold 45, so \(k + (k - 9) = 45\). Combine the like terms and solve for \(k\).
review.sol: Let \(k\) be the number of croissants on the kitchen tray. The window tray holds 9 fewer than the kitchen tray, so it holds \(k - 9\). The two trays together hold 45, which gives the equation $$k + (k - 9) = 45.$$ Combine the like terms to get \(2k - 9 = 45\). Add 9 to both sides to get \(2k = 54\), then divide by 2 to get \(k = 27\). The question asks for the kitchen tray, which is exactly the variable, so the answer is \(\boxed{27}\). Check it against the words. The kitchen tray has 27, the window tray has \(27 - 9 = 18\), and together they hold \(27 + 18 = 45\), which matches.

review.x: At a wildlife sanctuary, a thirsty llama drinks four times as much water in a day as a young goat does. On one hot afternoon the keeper measures their bowls and finds that together the two animals drank \(40\) liters. How many liters did the llama drink?
hints: Let \(g\) stand for the liters the goat drank. The llama drank four times as much, so the llama is \(4g\). Write an equation that says the two amounts add up to \(40\).
Your equation is \(g + 4g = 40\). Combine the like terms into \(5g\), solve for \(g\), and then remember the question asks about the llama, not the goat.
review.sol: Let \(g\) be the number of liters the goat drank. The llama drank four times as much, so the llama drank \(4g\). Together they drank \(40\) liters, which gives the equation $$g + 4g = 40.$$ Combine the like terms to get \(5g = 40\), then divide both sides by \(5\) to find \(g = 8\). That is the goat's amount, but the question asks how much the llama drank, so report \(4g = 4 \times 8 = 32\) liters. Check it against the words. The goat drank \(8\) liters and the llama drank \(32\) liters, the llama's \(32\) is four times the goat's \(8\), and \(8 + 32 = 40\), which matches the total. The llama drank \(\boxed{32}\) liters.

review.x: A robotics club is building a fleet of small machines for a demo. Every machine is either a roller, which moves on four wheels, or a hopper, which balances on two wheels. The club finished twenty-six machines in total, and when they counted every wheel in the workshop they reached eighty-four wheels. How many hoppers did the club build?
hints: Let \(c\) be the number of rollers. Then the number of hoppers is whatever is left out of 26, which is \(26 - c\). Rollers carry 4 wheels each and hoppers carry 2 wheels each.

Write the total wheels as \(4c + 2(26 - c) = 84\). Expand the left side and combine like terms, then solve for \(c\). Once you know \(c\), the question asks for hoppers, so report \(26 - c\), not \(c\) itself.
review.sol: Let \(c\) be the number of rollers. Since every machine is a roller or a hopper and there are 26 in all, the number of hoppers is \(26 - c\). Each roller has 4 wheels and each hopper has 2 wheels, so the wheel count is \(4c + 2(26 - c) = 84\). Expand the left side to get \(4c + 52 - 2c = 84\), which simplifies to \(2c + 52 = 84\). Subtract 52 from both sides to get \(2c = 32\), so \(c = 16\). That is the number of rollers, but the question asks for hoppers, and the hoppers are \(26 - c = 26 - 16 = 10\). So the club built \(\boxed{10}\) hoppers. Check it against the words. With 16 rollers and 10 hoppers that is \(16 + 10 = 26\) machines, and the wheels come to \(4 \times 16 + 2 \times 10 = 64 + 20 = 84\), which matches both counts.

review.x: A weather satellite is counting tiny ice crystals in a passing cloud. If you take a quarter of the crystal count and add five, you get exactly the same number as taking a sixth of the crystal count and adding nine. How many ice crystals are in the cloud?
hints: Let \(x\) be the number of ice crystals. A quarter of the count and add five is \(\frac{1}{4}x + 5\), and a sixth of the count and add nine is \(\frac{1}{6}x + 9\). Set them equal.
To get rid of the fractions, multiply every single term by \(12\), the smallest number both \(4\) and \(6\) divide into. That turns \(\frac{1}{4}x\) into \(3x\) and \(\frac{1}{6}x\) into \(2x\).
review.sol: Let \(x\) be the number of ice crystals in the cloud. A quarter of the count increased by five is \(\frac{1}{4}x + 5\), and a sixth of the count increased by nine is \(\frac{1}{6}x + 9\). The words say these are equal, so \(\frac{1}{4}x + 5 = \frac{1}{6}x + 9\). Multiply every term by \(12\) to clear the fractions, which gives \(3x + 60 = 2x + 108\). Subtract \(2x\) from both sides to get \(x + 60 = 108\), then subtract \(60\) to get \(x = 48\). The question asks for the number of ice crystals, so the answer is \(\boxed{48}\). Check against the words. A quarter of \(48\) is \(12\), and \(12 + 5 = 17\). A sixth of \(48\) is \(8\), and \(8 + 9 = 17\). Both sides give \(17\), so it fits.

review.x: A science club picks up \(12\) glow items for a night hike. Each one is either a small glow stick that costs \(\$3\) or a glow lantern that costs \(\$5\), and the club spends \(\$44\) in all. How many glow lanterns did the club buy?
hints: Let \(p\) be the number of glow sticks. Since there are \(12\) items in all, the number of lanterns has to be \(12 - p\). Each glow stick costs \(\$3\) and each lantern costs \(\$5\), and the whole total is \(\$44\), so write \(3p + 5(12 - p) = 44\).
Distribute the \(5\) to get \(3p + 60 - 5p = 44\), which simplifies to \(-2p + 60 = 44\). Solve that for \(p\), then remember the question asks for lanterns, which is \(12 - p\), not \(p\) itself.
review.sol: Let \(p\) be the number of glow sticks the club buys. There are \(12\) items in all, so the number of lanterns is \(12 - p\). Each glow stick costs \(\$3\) and each lantern costs \(\$5\), and the total spent is \(\$44\), which gives the equation $$3p + 5(12 - p) = 44.$$ Distribute the \(5\) across the group, \(5(12 - p) = 60 - 5p\), so the equation becomes \(3p + 60 - 5p = 44\). Combine the \(p\) terms, \(3p - 5p = -2p\), leaving \(-2p + 60 = 44\). Subtract \(60\) from both sides to get \(-2p = -16\), then divide both sides by \(-2\) to find \(p = 8\). So the club bought \(8\) glow sticks. The question asks for lanterns, not glow sticks, and the number of lanterns is \(12 - p = 12 - 8 = 4\). Check it against the words. With \(8\) glow sticks and \(4\) lanterns the count is \(8 + 4 = 12\) items, and the cost is \(3 \cdot 8 + 5 \cdot 4 = 24 + 20 = 44\) dollars, both of which match. So the club bought \(\boxed{4}\) glow lanterns.

review.x: A restored river boat sits in a small harbor museum, and the curator likes to quiz visitors about its age. Here is the clue on the plaque. Four years ago, the boat was only two thirds as old as it is right now. If \(t\) stands for the boat's age in years today, then four years ago its age was \(t - 4\), and that has to equal two thirds of its current age, so \(t - 4 = \tfrac{2}{3}t\). How old is the boat now? Type the number of years.
hints: The equation \(t - 4 = \tfrac{2}{3}t\) has a fraction in it. Multiplying every single term on both sides by \(3\) will clear that fraction and leave you whole numbers to work with.
review.sol: Let \(t\) be the boat's age in years today. Four years ago its age was \(t - 4\), and the plaque says that equals two thirds of its current age, which gives \(t - 4 = \tfrac{2}{3}t\). Clear the fraction by multiplying every term by \(3\), so \(3t - 12 = 2t\). Subtract \(2t\) from both sides to get \(t - 12 = 0\), and then \(t = 12\). The question asks how old the boat is now, and the variable \(t\) is exactly that age, so the answer is \(\boxed{12}\) years. Check it against the words. Four years ago the boat was \(12 - 4 = 8\) years old, and two thirds of its current age is \(\tfrac{2}{3}\times 12 = 8\). Those match, so \(12\) is right.

review.x: At a Saturday robotics meetup, two cousins, Priya and her younger cousin Dev, build a bot together. Their ages add up to \(44\) years, and Priya is exactly three times as old as Dev. How old is Priya in years?
hints: Let \(y\) stand for Dev's age, the younger one. Then Priya's age is \(3y\), since she is three times as old. Their ages add to \(44\).
Write \(y + 3y = 44\) and combine the like terms to get \(4y = 44\). Solve for \(y\), then remember the question asks for Priya, not Dev.
review.sol: Let \(y\) be Dev's age, the younger cousin. Priya is three times as old, so her age is \(3y\). Their ages add to \(44\), which gives the equation $$y + 3y = 44.$$ Combine the like terms to get \(4y = 44\), and divide both sides by \(4\) to find \(y = 11\). So Dev is \(11\). The question asks for Priya, whose age is \(3y = 3 \cdot 11 = 33\). Check it against the words. The ages add to \(11 + 33 = 44\), and \(33\) is three times \(11\), so both conditions hold. Priya is \(\boxed{33}\) years old.

review.x: A muralist is painting three side by side panels along a hallway wall, and each panel is one tile wider than the panel just before it. The three panels together span seventy-two tiles across. How many tiles wide is the widest panel?
hints: The three panel widths are consecutive integers. If you call the narrowest one \(n\), the next is \(n+1\) and the widest is \(n+2\).

Add the three widths and set the total equal to 72. That gives \(n + (n+1) + (n+2) = 72\), which simplifies to \(3n + 3 = 72\). Solve for \(n\) first, then remember the question asks for the widest panel, not the narrowest.
review.sol: Let \(n\) be the width of the narrowest panel in tiles. Each panel is one tile wider than the one before it, so the three widths are \(n\), \(n+1\), and \(n+2\). Together they span 72 tiles, so \(n + (n+1) + (n+2) = 72\). Combine the like terms to get \(3n + 3 = 72\). Subtract 3 from both sides for \(3n = 69\), then divide by 3 to get \(n = 23\). The narrowest panel is 23 tiles, the middle one is 24 tiles, and the widest is \(n + 2 = 25\) tiles. The question asks for the widest panel, so the answer is \(\boxed{25}\). Check it against the words. The three widths 23, 24, and 25 each grow by one tile, and \(23 + 24 + 25 = 72\), which matches the total span.

review.x: A locksmith is testing two padlocks whose secret codes are consecutive odd numbers. When she adds the two codes together she gets fifty-six. What is the larger of the two codes?
hints: Consecutive odd numbers always sit two apart, so if the smaller one is \(n\), the next odd one is \(n + 2\).
Add the two codes and set the sum equal to \(56\), giving \(n + (n + 2) = 56\). Solve for \(n\), then remember the question asks for the larger code, which is \(n + 2\).
review.sol: Let \(n\) be the smaller code. Because the two codes are consecutive odd numbers, they are two apart, so the larger code is \(n + 2\). The two codes add to \(56\), which gives the equation \(n + (n + 2) = 56\). Combining like terms, \(2n + 2 = 56\). Subtract \(2\) from both sides to get \(2n = 54\), then divide by \(2\) to find \(n = 27\). The smaller code is \(27\) and the larger code is \(n + 2 = 29\). The question asks for the larger code, so the answer is \(\boxed{29}\). Check against the words. The codes \(27\) and \(29\) are both odd, they are consecutive odd numbers since they differ by \(2\), and \(27 + 29 = 56\), which matches the total.

review.x: Two parking garages sit on the same block, and a driver wants to know which is cheaper. Garage A charges a flat $5 to enter plus $2 for every hour parked. Garage B charges a flat $11 to enter plus $1 for every hour parked. For short visits Garage A wins, and for long ones Garage B wins, so somewhere in the middle the two cost exactly the same. At how many hours of parking do the two garages charge the same amount?
hints: Let \(h\) be the number of hours. Garage A costs \(5 + 2h\) dollars and Garage B costs \(11 + h\) dollars. The two charge the same when those expressions are equal.
Set \(5 + 2h = 11 + h\). Subtract \(h\) from both sides to gather the variable, then subtract \(5\) to get \(h\) by itself.
review.sol: Let \(h\) be the number of hours parked. Garage A costs \(5 + 2h\) dollars and Garage B costs \(11 + h\) dollars. The two garages charge the same amount when those two costs are equal, so \(5 + 2h = 11 + h\). Subtract \(h\) from both sides to get \(5 + h = 11\), then subtract \(5\) from both sides to get \(h = 6\). The question asks at how many hours the costs match, which is exactly \(h\), so the answer is \(\boxed{6}\) hours. Check it against the words. At \(6\) hours Garage A costs \(5 + 2\cdot 6 = \$17\) and Garage B costs \(11 + 6 = \$17\), and the two amounts agree.

blocks.x: Every equation in this chapter ended on one exact number. There was a single value that made both sides match, the way one precise weight makes a scale hang perfectly level, and the moment you found it the puzzle was over. Real life is rarely that tidy. A recipe wants the oven to reach at least 350 degrees, not exactly 350, so 351 works and 400 works and 480 works too. A trampoline park lets you bounce only if you weigh under a posted limit, and a whole crowd of weights clears the rule. A bridge sign says trucks must stay below a certain height, and countless heights pass safely underneath. None of those answers is a lonely dot on the number line. Each one is a whole stretch of it, an entire run of numbers that all keep the promise. That kind of answer is called an inequality, and instead of asking when two sides are equal it asks when one side stays smaller or larger than the other. The good news is that you already own the tools for it. Solving an inequality leans on the very same balance moves you built up across 6.1 through 6.4, adding the same amount to both sides, taking the same amount off, multiplying or dividing both sides to peel a number away from \(x\). There is exactly one new twist waiting near the end, quiet and easy to miss, and learning to spot it is the whole adventure of this final lesson.

A solo ride at the fair only lets you board alone if your ticket count is strictly more than 5. In symbols, that rule is \(t > 5\). Five friends walk up to the gate holding \(3\), \(5\), \(7\), \(12\), and \(5.5\) tickets, since one of them split a half book with a sibling. The word strictly is doing real work here, because holding exactly \(5\) is not enough. You have to clear the line, not land on it. Of those five friends, how many are allowed to ride alone, meaning how many of those ticket counts satisfy \(t > 5\)?
hints: The rule is \(t > 5\). Go through the friends one at a time and ask whether each ticket count is bigger than \(5\). Watch the friend holding exactly \(5\), since \(> 5\) does not include \(5\) itself.
Mark each value yes or no for \(t > 5\). \(3\) is no, \(5\) is no because it only ties the line, \(7\) is yes, \(12\) is yes, and \(5.5\) is yes. Count the yeses.
blocks.sol: The rule is already solved for \(t\), so there is no rearranging to do and no sign to flip. The condition is simply \(t > 5\), which in words means the ticket count must be larger than \(5\), and the strict symbol \(>\) means \(5\) itself is left out. Now test each friend against \(t > 5\). For \(t = 3\), is \(3 > 5\)? No. For \(t = 5\), is \(5 > 5\)? No, because \(5\) is equal to \(5\), not greater, and strictly more means you cannot sit on the boundary. For \(t = 7\), is \(7 > 5\)? Yes. For \(t = 12\), is \(12 > 5\)? Yes. For \(t = 5.5\), is \(5.5 > 5\)? Yes, since half a ticket past \(5\) still clears the line. The solution range is every value above \(5\), written \(t > 5\), and the friend at exactly \(5\) falls just outside it. Counting the yeses gives \(7\), \(12\), and \(5.5\), which is three friends. So the number allowed to ride alone is \(\boxed{3}\).

blocks.x: So now I see what an inequality is really doing. An equals sign swore the two sides were the exact same number, which pinned me down to one lonely answer, but an inequality only swears that one side outranks the other, so a whole band of numbers can make it true at once, and any value that works counts as a solution, usually infinitely many of them. There are four symbols to keep straight, \(>\) for greater than and \(<\) for less than when the two sides are never allowed to tie, and \(\ge\) and \(\le\) when that little bar underneath also lets equality slip in, so the boundary itself gets to join. And because a range works instead of a single number, the picture is never one dot, it is a ray sliding off in one direction, or later a segment pinned between two ends.

blocks.cap: A hollow circle and a filled circle say two different things at the endpoint. The top line graphs \(x > 3\), so the circle at \(3\) is hollow because the sign is strict and \(3\) itself is left out, while the ray sweeps right over every value above \(3\) at once. The bottom line graphs \(x \le 7\), so the circle at \(7\) is filled because the sign allows equality and \(7\) is kept in, while the ray sweeps left through \(0\) and on over every value below \(7\). Same idea both times, a hollow ring drops the boundary and a filled dot holds onto it, and the arrow shades in the whole direction in one stroke.

blocks.x: Here is how I read one of these pictures now without second-guessing myself. The little circle at the end is a gate, and a hollow open circle means that exact number is locked out, I can creep as close to it as I want but never land on it, which is what the plain \(>\) and \(<\) signs are telling me. A filled closed circle swings the gate open and lets the endpoint in, because the or-equal part of \(\ge\) and \(\le\) says equality counts too. The arrow finishes the story in one sweep, carrying every number that works off in its direction, so the gate tells me the strictness and the arrow tells me which way the answers run.

A delivery cart already holds \(8\) crates, and the dock manager wants the running total to stay under \(20\) crates so the cart still rolls smoothly down the ramp. If \(x\) is how many more crates you may stack on, the rule is \(x + 8 < 20\). Solve it the same way you would solve \(x + 8 = 20\), by peeling \(8\) off both sides. The boundary you reach is strict, so a value sitting exactly on it does not count. What is the largest whole number of crates \(x\) can be?
hints: Make the first move you would make for an equation. Subtract \(8\) from both sides to get \(x\) alone. Adding or subtracting the same amount never flips an inequality, so the \(<\) sign stays pointing the same way. Then notice the bound is strict, which matters for a whole number answer.

Subtracting \(8\) from both sides gives \(x < 12\). Since \(x\) must be strictly less than \(12\), the value \(12\) itself is out. The largest whole number that still fits is the integer just below \(12\).
blocks.sol: Start with the rule from the problem, \(x + 8 < 20\). To get \(x\) by itself, subtract \(8\) from both sides, exactly the move you would make for the equation \(x + 8 = 20\). Subtracting the same amount from both sides shifts the values but never reverses the inequality, so there is no sign flip here and the \(<\) keeps pointing the same way. That leaves \(x < 20 - 8\), which is \(x < 12\). In words, the solution is every value strictly less than \(12\), written \(x < 12\). The bound is strict, so \(12\) is not allowed, you would need fewer than \(12\) more crates. The largest whole number strictly below \(12\) is \(11\). Check it. Loading \(11\) more crates gives \(11 + 8 = 19\), and \(19 < 20\) is true, while \(12\) more would give \(12 + 8 = 20\), and \(20 < 20\) is false, so \(11\) really is the top. The most crates you may stack on is \(\boxed{11}\).

blocks.x: A scuba diver's gauge shows her depth as a negative number, so a reading of \(-8\) means eight meters below the surface. Right now her reading is some value \(x\). She kicks upward and rises exactly 5 meters, and her new reading is \(x + 5\). For the safety check at this point, her new reading must be at least 2, which we write as \(x + 5 \ge 2\). Solving this tells you every starting reading that keeps her safe. What is the smallest value \(x\) can be?
hints: Treat the inequality just like an equation for the first move. To get \(x\) by itself, undo the \(+5\) by subtracting 5 from both sides. You only flip the inequality sign when you multiply or divide both sides by a negative, and subtracting does not do that, so the \(\ge\) stays pointing the same way.

Subtract 5 from both sides of \(x + 5 \ge 2\) and you get \(x \ge -3\). The sign is \(\ge\), which means \(-3\) itself is allowed, so the gate is closed. The smallest reading that still works is the boundary number \(-3\).
blocks.sol: Start with the safety condition \(x + 5 \ge 2\). You solve it exactly the way you solved equations all chapter, by undoing operations one at a time. Undo the \(+5\) by subtracting 5 from both sides, which gives \(x \ge -3\). No sign flip happens here, because the sign only reverses when you multiply or divide both sides by a negative, and subtracting a number never does that, so the \(\ge\) keeps pointing the same way. In words, her starting reading can be \(-3\) or anything greater. In symbols that is \(x \ge -3\). Because the sign is \(\ge\) and not \(>\), the boundary \(-3\) is itself allowed, so the gate is closed and \(-3\) is a value she is permitted to start from. The smallest value \(x\) can be is therefore the boundary number, \(-3\). $$\boxed{-3}$$

blocks.x: Here is the last new twist of the chapter, and it is a gentle one. Four identical sandbags are stacked on a single shelf, and the shelf is rated to hold at most \(26\) kilograms in total. If \(x\) is the weight of one bag in kilograms, then all four together weigh \(4x\), and "at most \(26\)" means \(4x \le 26\). This is solved exactly like an equation, you just keep the inequality sign instead of an equals sign. Divide both sides by the positive number \(4\) to get \(x\) by itself. What is the LARGEST value \(x\) can be? Give your answer as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: The bags share the load, so undo the \(4\) by dividing both sides by \(4\). You are dividing by a positive number here, so watch what happens to the inequality sign. When you divide by a positive, the sign keeps pointing the same way, it does not flip.

Divide both sides of \(4x \le 26\) by \(4\). The sign stays as \(\le\), giving \(x \le \tfrac{26}{4}\). Simplify \(\tfrac{26}{4}\) to lowest terms, and since the bound is "at most" the boundary itself is allowed, so the largest value is that boundary.
blocks.sol: Start from the setup \(4x \le 26\). The four bags together cannot top \(26\) kilograms, so their combined weight \(4x\) is at most \(26\). To isolate \(x\), divide both sides by \(4\). Because \(4\) is a positive number, the inequality keeps pointing the same direction, so there is no sign flip here. $$\frac{4x}{4} \le \frac{26}{4}$$ That leaves \(x \le \tfrac{26}{4}\), and \(\tfrac{26}{4}\) reduces to \(\tfrac{13}{2}\). So the solution range is every weight up to and including \(\tfrac{13}{2}\), in words "x is at most thirteen halves" and in symbols \(x \le \tfrac{13}{2}\). The word "most" means the boundary counts, the gate is closed, so \(\tfrac{13}{2}\) itself is allowed. The largest value \(x\) can be is therefore the boundary, \(\boxed{13/2}\) kilograms.

blocks.x: Here is the part that surprised me. Solving an inequality is the same game I already know, just with a \(>\) or a \(<\) where the \(=\) used to be. I tidy up each side, I undo a plus with a minus, I undo a times with a divide, and the bigger side stays the bigger side the whole way through, because adding or taking the same amount from both sides shifts them together and scaling both by the same positive number stretches them together. Every single move from 6.2 and 6.3 carries straight over. But there is exactly one move I have not dared try yet, scaling both sides by a negative number, and I have a feeling that one is about to break the pattern.

The pointed signs \(<\) and \(>\) are far younger than the numbers they compare. They first appeared in print in a book by Thomas Harriot, an English mathematician and explorer, that came out in 1631, a full decade after he had died, when an editor gathered his scattered notes into a volume titled Artis Analyticae Praxis. For a while other people pushed rival marks, and none of them were obvious winners, yet Harriot's symbols quietly took over for a friendly reason. The wide open end faces the larger amount and the pinched point aims at the smaller one, a built in hint about which way the comparison runs that students still lean on today. The rounded cousins \(\le\) and \(\ge\), which fold "or equal to" into a single mark, did not show up until much later, popularized by the French mathematician Pierre Bouguer in the seventeen hundreds.

Here is a statement nobody would argue with. \(3 < 8\). Three really is less than eight. Now do something to both sides at once. Multiply each side by \(-1\). The \(3\) becomes \(-3\) and the \(8\) becomes \(-8\). Picture those two new numbers on a number line. One of them sits farther to the left than the other, and the one farther left is the smaller one. So our two results are \(-3\) and \(-8\). Of those two values, which one is the LARGER?
hints: Put \(-3\) and \(-8\) on a number line and remember the rule from earlier chapters. Farther to the left means smaller, farther to the right means larger. Watch what happened to the order. The statement started as \(3 < 8\), with the left number smaller, but after the move the smaller one is no longer on the left.
Mark both numbers. \(-8\) is eight steps left of zero, and \(-3\) is only three steps left of zero, so \(-3\) sits to the RIGHT of \(-8\). The number on the right is the larger one. That is \(-3\).
blocks.sol: Start with a true statement, \(3 < 8\). Multiply both sides by \(-1\). The left side becomes \(-3\) and the right side becomes \(-8\). Here is the surprise. If you just copied the old direction you would write \(-3 < -8\), but that is false, because \(-8\) is farther left on the number line and is the smaller number. To keep the statement true the inequality sign has to flip. This is the sign flip. Multiplying or dividing both sides by a negative reverses the direction of the inequality, so the true statement is \(-3 > -8\). Reflecting both numbers across zero turned the order around. In words, after the flip \(-3\) is now the larger value and \(-8\) is the smaller one, and the relationship is \(-3 > -8\). The question asks for the larger of the two results, which is \(\boxed{-3}\).

blocks.cap: Start with something plainly true, \(3 < 8\). Multiplying both sides by \(-1\) sends every number to its opposite, which on the line is a reflection straight through zero. The point at \(3\) lands on \(-3\) and the point at \(8\) lands on \(-8\). But a reflection turns left into right, so \(8\), once the rightmost and largest, becomes \(-8\), now the leftmost and smallest. To keep the statement true the relationship has to read \(-3 > -8\). That single forced swap, from \(<\) to \(>\), is the whole reason the inequality sign flips whenever you multiply or divide both sides by a negative.

blocks.x: Here is the only new rule of the whole lesson, and it is the only thing that makes inequalities different from the equations I already know how to solve. The moment I multiply or divide both sides by a negative number, I have to flip the sign, so \(<\) becomes \(>\), \(\le\) becomes \(\ge\), and the same the other way, because flipping to the negative side reverses which value is bigger. Adding, subtracting, and multiplying or dividing by a positive never flip anything, so those moves are always safe. That means the entire skill comes down to one question I ask at the very end of my work, did I just divide by a negative, and if I did, I flip.

A freezer alarm watches a sensor reading \(x\) and trips whenever \(-5x\) climbs above \(17\). So the danger zone is exactly the inequality \(-5x > 17\). Every solving move you learned this chapter still works here, with one twist waiting in the wings. What is the largest integer \(x\) can be?
hints: Get \(x\) alone by dividing both sides by \(-5\). The moment you divide by a negative number, something has to happen to the inequality sign. Decide what it becomes before you read off the answer.

Dividing by \(-5\) flips \(>\) into \(<\), so \(x < -\frac{17}{5}\), which is \(x < -3.4\). The gate at \(-3.4\) is open, so \(-3.4\) itself is not allowed. Now name the largest integer that sits strictly to the left of \(-3.4\) on the number line.
blocks.sol: Start with \(-5x > 17\). To free \(x\), divide both sides by \(-5\). Here is the one twist that makes inequalities different from equations. Dividing both sides by a negative number reverses the inequality, so the \(>\) flips to \(<\). $$x < \frac{17}{-5} = -\frac{17}{5} = -3.4$$ In words, \(x\) must be smaller than negative three and four tenths. In symbols that is \(x < -\frac{17}{5}\). The bound is strict, so the gate at \(-\frac{17}{5}\) is open and \(-3.4\) itself is shut out. The question asks for the largest integer that fits. Walking left from \(-3.4\) on the number line, the first integer you reach is \(-4\), since \(-3\) sits to the right of \(-3.4\) and is too big. A quick check, \(-5 \cdot (-4) = 20\), and \(20 > 17\) is true, while \(-5 \cdot (-3) = 15\), and \(15 > 17\) is false. The largest integer \(x\) can be is \(\boxed{-4}\).

blocks.x: Here is the toughest inequality of the chapter, and it pulls together everything you can already do. The variable appears on both sides, and there is a grouping symbol to clear first. Solve \(3(2 - x) > 2x + 1\) for \(x\). Stay alert, because at some point you may have to divide by a negative number, and you remember what that does. Once you find the range, answer this one question. What is the largest integer \(x\) can be?
hints: Start exactly the way you would for an equation. Distribute the \(3\) across the parentheses to get \(6 - 3x\) on the left, then move the variable terms to one side and the numbers to the other. Keep one eye out for a moment where you divide by a negative, since that is the only thing here that behaves differently from an equation.

After distributing you have \(6 - 3x > 2x + 1\). Subtract \(2x\) from both sides to reach \(6 - 5x > 1\), then subtract \(6\) to get \(-5x > -5\). Now divide both sides by \(-5\), and because that divisor is negative, FLIP the sign. That gives \(x < 1\). The bound is strict, so \(1\) itself is not allowed. Name the largest whole number that still sits below \(1\).
blocks.sol: Treat this like any both-sides equation right up until the very last step. First distribute the \(3\) across the parentheses. $$3(2 - x) > 2x + 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 6 - 3x > 2x + 1$$ Now gather the variable terms. Subtract \(2x\) from both sides. $$6 - 5x > 1$$ Subtract \(6\) from both sides to isolate the variable term. $$-5x > -5$$ Here is the one move that makes inequalities different. To get \(x\) by itself you divide both sides by \(-5\), and dividing by a negative number FLIPS the inequality sign. The \(>\) becomes \(<\). $$x < 1$$ In words, every number strictly less than \(1\) makes the original statement true, and \(1\) itself does not, because the bound is strict. In symbols the solution is \(x < 1\). The question asks for the largest integer \(x\) can be. Since \(1\) is shut out by the strict sign, the largest integer that still fits is the whole number just below it, which is \(0\). A quick check confirms it. Putting \(x = 0\) into the original gives \(3(2) = 6\) on the left and \(1\) on the right, and \(6 > 1\) is true, while \(x = 1\) gives \(3\) on the left and \(3\) on the right, and \(3 > 3\) is false. The largest integer is \(\boxed{0}\).

blocks.x: Here is the trickiest one in the chapter, and it folds together everything. Solve \(5 - 2x - 8 > 4 - x\). Notice the left side has two number pieces sitting apart, so tidy it first, then move the variables together. Watch closely near the end, because one move may force the sign to turn around. Once you have the solution range, answer this. What is the largest integer \(x\) can be?
hints: Start by combining the two number terms on the left, since \(5 - 8\) is a single value. That turns the left side into \(-3 - 2x\). Then gather the variables on one side the same way you did when solving equations. Keep an eye out for the step that might flip the inequality.

After tidying you have \(-3 - 2x > 4 - x\). Add \(x\) to both sides to get \(-3 - x > 4\), then add \(3\) to both sides to get \(-x > 7\). Now multiply both sides by \(-1\), and because the multiplier is negative you must reverse the sign, giving \(x < -7\). The bound is strict, so \(-7\) itself is not allowed. The largest integer that fits is the whole number just below \(-7\).
blocks.sol: Begin by tidying the left side. The numbers \(5\) and \(-8\) combine, and \(5 - 8 = -3\), so the inequality becomes \(-3 - 2x > 4 - x\). Now gather the variables. Add \(x\) to both sides, and \(-2x + x = -x\), which gives \(-3 - x > 4\). Add \(3\) to both sides to clear the number on the left, leaving \(-x > 7\). The last move is to turn \(-x\) into \(x\) by multiplying both sides by \(-1\). Multiplying by a negative reverses the inequality, so the sign flips and the result is \(x < -7\). In words, \(x\) is any number strictly less than negative seven, written \(x < -7\). The gate at \(-7\) is open, so \(-7\) does not count, and the largest integer below it is \(-8\). A quick check confirms it, since putting \(x = -8\) into the original gives \(13 > 12\), which is true, while \(x = -7\) gives \(11 > 11\), which is false. So the largest integer \(x\) can be is \(\boxed{-8}\).

blocks.cap: A compound inequality traps the variable between two bounds at the same moment, so the picture is a finite segment instead of an endless ray. The left gate at \(-3\) is a closed dot because \(-3 \le x\) lets \(x\) actually be \(-3\), while the right gate at \(2\) is an open dot because \(x < 2\) never lets \(x\) reach \(2\). Together they say \(-3 \le x < 2\), the gold bar between the gates. Counting the integer dots caught inside gives a finite answer, here the five values \(-3, -2, -1, 0, 1\).

blocks.x: Here is the trickiest kind of inequality, a compound one with the variable trapped in the middle and wearing a negative coefficient. Solve \(1 < 5 - 2x \le 11\). The rule that carried you through this whole chapter still holds, whatever you do to one part you do to all three parts at the same time, so the middle stays fenced in on both sides. Once you have x cornered, count how many integers make the whole thing true. How many integers satisfy the inequality?
hints: Peel away the constant from all three parts first. Subtract \(5\) from the left, the middle, and the right, and you should land on \(-4 < -2x \le 6\). Now you have to divide every part by \(-2\), and that negative is about to do something to the signs, so stay alert.

Dividing all three parts by \(-2\) flips BOTH inequality signs, which gives \(2 > x \ge -3\). Read that the tidy way around and it says \(-3 \le x < 2\), closed at \(-3\) and open at \(2\). Now just list the integers from \(-3\) up to but not including \(2\) and count them.
blocks.sol: Treat all three parts as one moving system. Start from \(1 < 5 - 2x \le 11\) and subtract \(5\) from every part, which gives \(-4 < -2x \le 6\). Now divide every part by \(-2\). Because \(-2\) is negative, both inequality signs FLIP, and that sign flip is the one twist this whole topic adds. Dividing turns it into \(2 > x \ge -3\). Reading that from small to large, the solution range is \(-3 \le x < 2\), in words every number from \(-3\) included up to \(2\) but not reaching \(2\). The gate at \(-3\) is closed and the gate at \(2\) is open. Quick checks confirm the boundaries. At \(x = -3\), \(5 - 2(-3) = 11\), and \(11 \le 11\) holds, so \(-3\) is in. At \(x = 2\), \(5 - 2(2) = 1\), and \(1 < 1\) is false, so \(2\) is out. The integers in the range are \(-3, -2, -1, 0, 1\), which is \(5\) of them. The number of integers that satisfy the inequality is \(\boxed{5}\).

blocks.x: Here is the last thing the chapter taught me, and it ties the whole knot. A compound inequality like \(4 < x \le 9\) is really two demands holding at the same instant, a floor underneath and a ceiling overhead, so instead of an endless ray the answer is a fenced-in stretch with both ends marked. Solving it is the exact balance work I already trust, except now there are three parts standing side by side, so whatever I do I have to do to the left, the middle, and the right all together or the picture stops being true. And the one twist from earlier never went away. The moment I scale all three parts by a negative, both ends flip their direction at once, the floor and the ceiling trading places in the same breath.

Priya runs a little booth at a weekend craft fair. The booth space costs a flat \(45\) dollars for the day no matter what. She makes scented erasers that cost her \(3\) dollars each to produce and sell for \(8\) dollars, so every eraser puts \(5\) dollars in her pocket after she covers what it cost to make. Profit means the money that comes in minus every cost, including that booth fee. If she sells \(n\) erasers, her profit is \(5n - 45\) dollars, and she wants that profit to be at least \(200\) dollars, so the goal is \(5n - 45 \ge 200\). What is the fewest whole erasers Priya must sell to reach her goal?
hints: Each eraser adds \(5\) to her profit, but the \(45\) booth fee gets subtracted once. Start by undoing that subtraction. Add \(45\) to both sides so the \(n\) term sits alone, and notice you are only adding and dividing by positive numbers here, so the \(\ge\) sign stays pointing the same way the whole time.

After adding \(45\) you get \(5n \ge 245\). Divide both sides by \(5\), a positive number, so no flip, and you land on \(n \ge 49\). Since she cannot sell part of an eraser and \(49\) is already a whole number that is allowed, the fewest is \(49\) itself.
blocks.sol: Set up the goal from the profit. Selling \(n\) erasers earns \(5n\) dollars after production cost, and the \(45\) dollar booth fee comes out once, so the profit is \(5n - 45\). She wants it to be at least \(200\), which gives \(5n - 45 \ge 200\). Solve it exactly the way you solved equations in this chapter, with one thing to watch. First add \(45\) to both sides to free the \(n\) term, and that gives \(5n \ge 245\). Now divide both sides by \(5\). Because \(5\) is positive, the inequality sign does not flip, it keeps pointing the same way, and you get \(n \ge 49\). In words, she meets her goal as long as she sells \(49\) or more erasers, written \(n \ge 49\). Check the boundary. At \(n = 49\) the profit is \(5 \cdot 49 - 45 = 245 - 45 = 200\) dollars, which hits the goal exactly since "at least" includes \(200\). At \(n = 48\) the profit is \(5 \cdot 48 - 45 = 240 - 45 = 195\) dollars, which falls short. She cannot sell a fraction of an eraser, and \(49\) is a whole number that already works, so the fewest whole erasers she must sell is \(\boxed{49}\).

blocks.x: Your phone is at \(95\) percent battery when movie night starts. Every background app you forget to close quietly drains \(4\) percent overnight, so if you leave \(a\) apps running, the battery in the morning is \(95 - 4a\) percent. You want to wake up with at least \(40\) percent left, which means \(95 - 4a \ge 40\). What is the most whole apps you can leave running?
hints: Get the \(a\) term alone first. Subtract \(95\) from both sides of \(95 - 4a \ge 40\), and notice that the number multiplying \(a\) is negative, which is your warning sign that something special is about to happen.

After subtracting you have \(-4a \ge -55\). Divide both sides by \(-4\), and because you divided by a negative number you must flip the \(\ge\) into a \(\le\). That gives \(a \le \tfrac{55}{4} = 13.75\). Apps come in whole numbers, so find the biggest whole number that fits.
blocks.sol: Start from the condition that the morning battery stays at least \(40\) percent, so \(95 - 4a \ge 40\). Subtract \(95\) from both sides to peel away the starting charge, which leaves \(-4a \ge -55\). Now divide both sides by \(-4\) to free \(a\). Dividing by a negative number flips the inequality, so the \(\ge\) becomes a \(\le\), and you get \(a \le \tfrac{55}{4}\). Since \(\tfrac{55}{4} = 13.75\), the solution range is every value of \(a\) that is at most \(13.75\), written \(a \le 13.75\). Apps come in whole numbers, so \(a\) has to be a whole number no bigger than \(13.75\), and the largest such whole number is \(13\). Check the edges. With \(13\) apps the battery is \(95 - 4 \cdot 13 = 43\) percent, comfortably above \(40\). With \(14\) apps it is \(95 - 4 \cdot 14 = 39\) percent, which slips under the line. So the most whole apps you can leave running is \(\boxed{13}\).

blocks.x: Three runners hand off in a relay, and we score each leg so that a bigger number means faster. From the timing sheet we know two facts. Priya was faster than Quinn, written \(P > Q\), and Quinn was at least as fast as Rafe, written \(Q \ge R\), where "at least as fast" leaves room for an exact tie. Now a teammate scribbles down five claims about who beat whom, \(P > R\), \(P \ge R\), \(P > Q\), \(Q > R\), and \(R > P\). Using only the two facts above and the way inequalities chain when they point the same direction, some of these claims must be true no matter the exact scores, and some could go either way. How many of the five claims are forced to be true?
hints: Line the two facts up so the arrows point the same way, \(P > Q \ge R\). Anything you can read straight off this chain by following greater-than signs from left to right is forced. Watch the spot where \(Q\) meets \(R\), because that link is \(\ge\) and not a strict \(>\).

Forced because they read straight off the chain, \(P > Q\) is given outright, \(P > R\) jumps across the whole chain, and \(P \ge R\) is a weaker true statement that \(P > R\) already guarantees. Not forced, \(Q > R\) fails whenever Quinn and Rafe tie since that link is only \(\ge\), and \(R > P\) is actually backwards. Count the forced ones.
blocks.sol: Start by stacking the two given facts so their inequality signs face the same direction. Since \(P > Q\) and \(Q \ge R\), the whole picture becomes one chain, \(P > Q \ge R\). Reading a chain like this is the heart of the problem. When every link points the same way you may follow them across and combine them, and a strict \(>\) anywhere along the chain keeps the combined result strict. Now test each of the five claims against the chain. The claim \(P > Q\) is one of the two facts we were handed, so it is forced to be true. The claim \(P > R\) asks whether Priya beats Rafe, and following the chain from \(P\) past \(Q\) all the way to \(R\) crosses the strict \(>\) between \(P\) and \(Q\), so the combined comparison stays strict and \(P > R\) is forced. The claim \(P \ge R\) says Priya is at least as fast as Rafe, which is a weaker statement than \(P > R\), and since the stronger \(P > R\) is already guaranteed, \(P \ge R\) is forced too. That is three forced so far. The claim \(Q > R\) is not forced, because the link between \(Q\) and \(R\) is only \(\ge\), so Quinn and Rafe are allowed to tie. If \(Q = R\) then \(Q > R\) is false, which means this claim can fail. The claim \(R > P\) is not forced either, it is actually backwards, since the chain puts \(P\) at the fast end and \(R\) at the slow end, so \(R > P\) is impossible. Stating the result in words, the forced claims are \(P > Q\), \(P > R\), and \(P \ge R\), while \(Q > R\) and \(R > P\) are not forced. Counting the forced claims gives \(3\). \(\boxed{3}\)

blocks.x: Here is the last thing I worked out. When a bunch of comparisons all lean the same way, I can hook them into one long chain and just read straight across it, so if one amount tops a second and that second is at least as big as a third, then the first has to top the third, no measuring the gap needed. The one thing I have to watch is whether the links are strict. If even a single link is a real beat with no tie allowed, that strictness travels the whole length and the ends come out strictly apart, but if every link is only an at-least, I cannot promise the ends are truly different, because a quiet tie could sneak through every step and leave them equal.

Time to bring the whole chapter together. A lab is cooling a small metal sample to study how it behaves once frozen. The sample starts at \(22\) degrees Celsius, and the cooler pulls its temperature down by \(1.5\) degrees every minute. The catch is that the metal does not freeze the instant it reaches the line. It freezes only once its temperature drops strictly below \(-8\) degrees. After \(m\) minutes the temperature is \(22 - 1.5m\), so the freezing condition is the inequality \(22 - 1.5m < -8\). What is the FEWEST whole minutes the lab must wait until the sample freezes?
hints: Solve \(22 - 1.5m < -8\) just like a normal equation. Subtract \(22\) from both sides to get \(-1.5m < -30\). Now watch closely, because your next move divides both sides by a negative number, and that does something to the inequality sign.

Divide both sides by \(-1.5\). Dividing by a negative flips the sign, so \(-1.5m < -30\) turns into \(m > 20\). The bound is strict, so \(m = 20\) does not count. Check it. At \(20\) minutes the temperature is exactly \(-8\), which is not below the line yet, and at \(21\) minutes it is \(-9.5\), which finally is. So the answer is the first whole number bigger than \(20\).
blocks.sol: Set up the freezing condition. After \(m\) minutes the temperature is \(22 - 1.5m\), and the sample freezes once that drops strictly below \(-8\), so the inequality is \(22 - 1.5m < -8\). Solve it the same way you solve any linear equation, one step at a time. Subtract \(22\) from both sides to get \(-1.5m < -30\). Now divide both sides by \(-1.5\). This is the one twist of the whole chapter. Dividing both sides by a negative number flips the inequality, so the \(<\) becomes \(>\) and you get \(m > 20\). In words, the sample is frozen for every number of minutes greater than \(20\), and because the bound is strict, \(20\) itself is not included. The gate at \(20\) is open. Now check the two values right around that gate. At \(m = 20\) the temperature is \(22 - 1.5 \cdot 20 = -8\), which is exactly on the line and not yet below it, so the sample has not frozen. At \(m = 21\) the temperature is \(22 - 1.5 \cdot 21 = -9.5\), which is below \(-8\), so it has frozen. The fewest whole minutes is the first whole number that satisfies \(m > 20\), which is \(\boxed{21}\).

review.x: A rewards account stays open as long as its balance \(w\) satisfies \(w \ge -4\), because the company forgives a small overdraft. Five accounts have balances of \(-6\), \(-4\), \(-3\), \(0\), and \(5\). How many of these accounts satisfy \(w \ge -4\)?
hints: The sign is \(\ge\), so test each balance against \(-4\). Remember that the bound itself is allowed because of the equals part.
Going up the list, \(-6\) sits below \(-4\), but \(-4\) itself counts. Check whether each of \(-4\), \(-3\), \(0\), and \(5\) lands at or above \(-4\).
review.sol: The rule keeps an account open when \(w \ge -4\), which in words is every balance at or above \(-4\). There is no negative multiplier here, so nothing flips. I just check each balance. \(-6\) is below \(-4\), so it fails. \(-4\) equals \(-4\), and the \(\ge\) sign includes equality, so it passes. \(-3\), \(0\), and \(5\) are all above \(-4\), so they pass too. The passing balances are \(-4\), \(-3\), \(0\), and \(5\), which is a count of \(\boxed{4}\).

review.x: A handheld scanner on an assembly line is set to keep a reading \(m\) only when \(m < 7\), and it tosses out anything that hits or passes the threshold. One shift produces these five readings, \(2\), \(7\), \(6.9\), \(8\), and \(-1\). Notice that the threshold \(7\) sits right on the boundary, and a strict inequality does not include its own boundary. How many of the five readings does the scanner keep?
hints: The rule is \(m < 7\) with a strict less-than, so check each reading against \(7\) one at a time. A reading is kept only when it lands strictly below \(7\).
The trap is the reading that equals \(7\). Since \(7 < 7\) is false, that one is rejected. Decide each of the other four, then count how many were kept.
review.sol: The keep rule is the strict inequality \(m < 7\). A strict less-than is true only when the reading sits below \(7\) and never when it equals \(7\), so the boundary value itself is rejected. Test each reading. For \(2\) we have \(2 < 7\), true, kept. For \(7\) we have \(7 < 7\), false, rejected, since a number is not less than itself. For \(6.9\) we have \(6.9 < 7\), true, kept. For \(8\) we have \(8 < 7\), false, rejected. For \(-1\) we have \(-1 < 7\), true, kept. The kept readings are \(2\), \(6.9\), and \(-1\), which describes the range \(m < 7\), every reading strictly below seven. That is \(3\) readings. The single asked value is the count, \(\boxed{3}\).

review.x: A small ferry can carry at most a fixed amount of weight before the warning light blinks. Right now the deck already holds \(13\) crates, and the captain wants to load \(x\) more identical crates without the light coming on. The rule the captain follows is \(x + 13 < 25\). Solve it to find every value \(x\) can take, then answer the single question. What is the LARGEST INTEGER number of extra crates \(x\) the captain can load?
hints: Get \(x\) by itself the same way you would in an equation. Subtract \(13\) from both sides, and since you are not multiplying or dividing by a negative, the \(<\) symbol stays pointing the same way.
After you find \(x < 12\), remember the bound is strict, so \(12\) itself does not work. The largest whole number that is still strictly below \(12\) is the one just under it.
review.sol: Start from \(x + 13 < 25\). Subtract \(13\) from both sides to undo the addition. Because you are subtracting a number and not multiplying or dividing by a negative, there is no sign flip and the inequality keeps pointing the same way. That leaves \(x < 12\). In words, x can be any value strictly less than twelve, written \(x < 12\). The bound is strict, so \(12\) is not allowed, only values below it. The captain loads whole crates, so the largest integer strictly below \(12\) is \(11\). Check it. Loading \(11\) gives \(11 + 13 = 24\), and \(24 < 25\) is true, while loading \(12\) gives \(12 + 13 = 25\), and \(25 < 25\) is false. The largest integer number of extra crates is \(\boxed{11}\).

review.x: A submarine drone is hovering at some depth and slowly rising. Its depth reading in meters below the surface follows the rule \(x - 9 \ge -2\), where \(x\) is the dial setting the pilot chooses. The pilot wants the smallest dial setting that still keeps the reading valid. What is the smallest value \(x\) can be?
hints: You want \(x\) alone. The 9 is being subtracted, so undo it by adding 9 to both sides. Nothing is multiplied by a negative here, so the inequality sign does not flip.
After adding 9 you get \(x \ge 7\). The \(\ge\) means \(x\) is allowed to equal 7, so 7 itself is a legal value. The smallest allowed value is the boundary.
review.sol: Start with \(x - 9 \ge -2\). Add 9 to both sides to get \(x\) by itself. The left side becomes \(x\) and the right side becomes \(-2 + 9\), which is \(7\). So \(x \ge 7\). Adding a number never flips an inequality, so the \(\ge\) stays pointing the same way and there is no sign flip to worry about. In words, \(x\) can be 7 or any number bigger than 7. In symbols that range is \(x \ge 7\). Because the bound is closed, 7 itself is allowed, so the smallest value \(x\) can be is exactly 7. The smallest value is \(\boxed{7}\).

review.x: A community garden hands out water from one big tank, and the rule keeps things fair. Each gardener may carry away \(8\) liters per visit, and a single permit slip is good for a total of \(28\) liters across all the visits it covers. If \(x\) stands for the number of visits a gardener makes on one permit, then the water taken is \(8x\) liters, and staying within the permit means \(8x \le 28\). The gardener wants to stretch one permit as far as it will go. What is the LARGEST value \(x\) can be? Give a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: You have \(8x \le 28\). To get \(x\) by itself, divide both sides by \(8\). Since \(8\) is positive, the inequality sign stays pointing the same way, no flip.
Dividing gives \(x \le \frac{28}{8}\). Simplify that fraction to lowest terms. Because the bound is \(\le\), the boundary value itself is allowed, so the largest \(x\) is exactly that boundary.
review.sol: Start from the permit rule \(8x \le 28\). To isolate \(x\), divide both sides by \(8\). The number \(8\) is positive, so the inequality sign does not flip and keeps pointing the same way. That gives \(x \le \frac{28}{8}\). Now simplify \(\frac{28}{8}\) by dividing top and bottom by \(4\), which gives \(\frac{7}{2}\). So the solution range is every \(x\) that is at most seven halves, written \(x \le \frac{7}{2}\). Because the sign is \(\le\) and not a strict \(<\), the boundary \(\frac{7}{2}\) is included in the range, so it is a closed upper bound. The largest value \(x\) can be is that boundary itself, \(\frac{7}{2}\). Check it. At \(x = \frac{7}{2}\) the water taken is \(8 \cdot \frac{7}{2} = 28\) liters, which lands right on the limit and is allowed, while any larger \(x\) would push past \(28\) and break the rule. So the answer is \(\boxed{7/2}\).

review.x: A bakery sells cookies in identical packs. Each pack costs \(7\) dollars, and Priya wants to spend strictly more than \(50\) dollars so she clears the free shipping bar, which only kicks in above fifty dollars. If she buys \(x\) packs, her spend is \(7x\) dollars, so she needs \(7x > 50\). What is the SMALLEST INTEGER number of packs \(x\) can be?
hints: Divide both sides by \(7\). Since \(7\) is positive, the inequality sign stays pointing the same way, so you get \(x > \frac{50}{7}\).
\(\frac{50}{7}\) is about \(7.14\). The sign is strictly greater than, so \(x\) must be above that, not equal to it. Pick the first whole number that clears \(7.14\).
review.sol: Start with \(7x > 50\). To undo the multiplication by \(7\), divide both sides by \(7\). Because \(7\) is positive, the inequality direction does not change, so there is no sign flip here. That gives \(x > \frac{50}{7}\). The fraction \(\frac{50}{7}\) is about \(7.14\), so in words \(x\) is any number strictly greater than \(\frac{50}{7}\), written \(x > \frac{50}{7}\). Since the bound is strict, \(x\) cannot equal \(7.14\), and it certainly cannot be \(7\) because \(7 \times 7 = 49\), which is not more than \(50\). The first whole number above \(7.14\) is \(8\), and checking it gives \(7 \times 8 = 56 > 50\), which works. So the smallest integer number of packs is \(\boxed{8}\).

review.x: A deep sea sensor is dropping through cold water, and its temperature reading \(x\) (in degrees) must satisfy \(-6x < 30\). Solve this inequality, then answer the single question. What is the smallest integer \(x\) can be?
hints: Divide both sides by \(-6\) to get \(x\) by itself. Watch what dividing by a negative does to the inequality sign.
After the flip you have \(x > -5\), a strict lower bound. Since \(-5\) itself is not allowed, find the first whole number that sits just above it.
review.sol: Start with \(-6x < 30\). To free \(x\) you divide both sides by \(-6\). Dividing by a negative flips the inequality, so the \(<\) becomes \(>\), and you get \(x > -5\). In words, \(x\) must be strictly greater than \(-5\). The boundary \(-5\) is not included, so any value bigger than \(-5\) works. Check it. At \(x = -4\) the left side is \(-6 \cdot (-4) = 24\), and \(24 < 30\) is true. At \(x = -5\) the left side is \(30\), and \(30 < 30\) is false, so \(-5\) really is shut out. The integers allowed are \(-4, -3, -2, \dots\), and the smallest of these is \(-4\). So the smallest integer \(x\) can be is \(\boxed{-4}\).

review.x: A frost alarm in a greenhouse trips whenever \(-5x\) climbs above \(12\), where \(x\) is the overnight temperature in a special unit that runs colder as it drops. Solve \(-5x > 12\) to find the temperatures that trip the alarm. What is the LARGEST INTEGER value \(x\) can be?
hints: Divide both sides by \(-5\) to get \(x\) alone. The moment you divide by a negative number, the \(>\) has to turn into \(<\).
After the flip you have \(x < -\tfrac{12}{5}\), which is \(x < -2.4\). Now walk left from \(-2.4\) on the number line. The first whole number you land on is the largest integer that still works.
review.sol: Start with \(-5x > 12\). To free \(x\), divide both sides by \(-5\). Dividing by a negative number flips the inequality, so the \(>\) becomes \(<\). That gives \(x < \frac{12}{-5}\), which simplifies to \(x < -\frac{12}{5}\). As a decimal that boundary is \(-2.4\). So the alarm trips for every temperature strictly below \(-2.4\), in words "x is less than negative two and four tenths" and in symbols \(x < -2.4\). The boundary itself does not count, since the sign is strict. The largest integer that sits to the left of \(-2.4\) on the number line is \(-3\), because \(-2\) is bigger than \(-2.4\) and fails, while \(-3\) is smaller and works. Check it. Plugging in \(-3\) gives \(-5 \cdot (-3) = 15\), and \(15 > 12\) is true, while \(-2\) gives \(10\), and \(10 > 12\) is false. So the largest integer \(x\) can be is \(\boxed{-3}\).

review.x: A vending machine charges \(4\) tokens for each cup of cocoa and keeps \(5\) tokens as a one time deposit. Mara wants the machine to credit her loyalty account, which only happens once it has counted more than \(19\) tokens past that deposit in a single visit. If \(x\) is the number of cups she buys, the tokens counted past the deposit are \(4x - 5\), so she needs \(4x - 5 > 19\). What is the SMALLEST INTEGER number of cups \(x\) she can buy?
hints: Add \(5\) to both sides to clear the deposit, then divide by \(4\). Since \(4\) is positive, the inequality sign does not flip.
You will land on \(x > 6\), a strict bound, so \(6\) itself is not enough. Step up to the next whole number.
review.sol: Start from \(4x - 5 > 19\) and add \(5\) to both sides, which gives \(4x > 24\). Now divide both sides by \(4\). Because \(4\) is positive, the inequality sign stays the same, so there is no flip here. That leaves \(x > 6\). In words, Mara needs strictly more than six cups, written \(x > 6\). The bound is strict, so six cups land exactly on \(19\) tokens past the deposit, which is not more than \(19\). The smallest whole number greater than \(6\) is \(7\), and seven cups give \(4(7) - 5 = 23\), comfortably past the threshold. So the smallest integer number of cups is \(\boxed{7}\).

review.x: A deep-sea drone starts the dive with a battery reading of \(10\) units, and each minute spent running its lamp drains the battery by \(4\) units, so after \(x\) minutes the reading is \(10 - 4x\). The pilot will only keep the lamp on while the reading stays at \(-6\) or above, which gives the inequality \(10 - 4x \ge -6\). Solve it for \(x\), then answer this. What is the LARGEST value \(x\) can be?
hints: Get the \(x\) term alone first. Subtract \(10\) from both sides of \(10 - 4x \ge -6\) to reach \(-4x \ge -16\).
Now divide both sides by \(-4\). Dividing by a negative number flips the inequality sign, so \(\ge\) becomes \(\le\). The largest allowed value is the boundary itself, since the bound is included.
review.sol: Start with \(10 - 4x \ge -6\). Subtract \(10\) from both sides to get \(-4x \ge -16\). Now divide both sides by \(-4\). Because you are dividing by a negative number, the inequality sign flips, so \(\ge\) turns into \(\le\), and you get \(x \le 4\). In words, \(x\) can be any value of \(4\) or less, which is a closed upper bound that includes \(4\) itself. The question asks for the largest value \(x\) can be, and since \(4\) is allowed, that largest value is \(\boxed{4}\). Check the boundary. At \(x = 4\) the reading is \(10 - 4\cdot 4 = -6\), which is exactly \(-6\) or above, so it works. At \(x = 5\) the reading is \(10 - 4\cdot 5 = -10\), which is below \(-6\), so it fails. The cutoff sits right at \(4\).

review.x: A drone hobbyist is comparing two ways its battery drains over a flight. In one mode the charge starts at \(7\) units and drops by \(1\) unit for every minute \(x\) it stays aloft, so the remaining charge is \(7 - x\). In a second mode the meter instead reads \(2x + 1\), climbing as the rotors work harder. She wants to know for how long the first reading beats the second, meaning when \(7 - x > 2x + 1\). Solve that inequality. What is the LARGEST INTEGER \(x\) can be?
hints: Start by gathering the \(x\) terms on one side. Subtract \(2x\) from both sides of \(7 - x > 2x + 1\) so the right side has no \(x\) left. You should reach \(7 - 3x > 1\).

Now subtract \(7\) from both sides to get \(-3x > -6\). To finish you divide both sides by \(-3\), and dividing by a negative flips the inequality. Once you find the strict upper bound, the largest integer is the biggest whole number that still sits strictly below it.
review.sol: The machinery is the same as solving an equation, with one twist waiting at the end. Begin with \(7 - x > 2x + 1\) and pull the variable to one side by subtracting \(2x\) from both sides, which gives \(7 - 3x > 1\). Subtract \(7\) from both sides to isolate the \(x\) term, leaving \(-3x > -6\). Here is the twist. To free \(x\) you divide both sides by \(-3\), and dividing by a negative number reverses the inequality, so the \(>\) flips to \(<\) and you get \(x < 2\). In words, the first reading wins for every flight time strictly less than \(2\) minutes, which in symbols is \(x < 2\). Since \(2\) itself is not included, the largest integer that fits is the biggest whole number below \(2\). A quick check confirms it. At \(x = 1\) the readings are \(6 > 3\), which is true, while at \(x = 2\) they tie at \(5 > 5\), which is false. So the largest integer \(x\) can be is \(\boxed{1}\).

review.x: A mosaic artist works in identical tile panels. For each batch she plans \(x\) panels, but the first \(2\) panels of every batch are always practice runs that get scrapped, so a batch of \(x\) panels uses \(3(x - 2)\) ounces of grout. Her grout bucket holds \(18\) ounces, and she wants one batch to fit inside what the bucket holds. Solve \(3(x - 2) \le 18\). What is the LARGEST value \(x\) can be?
hints: Distribute the \(3\) across the parentheses to get \(3x - 6 \le 18\), then add \(6\) to both sides.
You reach \(3x \le 24\). Divide both sides by \(3\). Since \(3\) is positive, the inequality sign does not flip.
review.sol: Distribute the \(3\) to get \(3x - 6 \le 18\). Add \(6\) to both sides, which gives \(3x \le 24\). Now divide both sides by \(3\). Because \(3\) is positive, there is no sign flip, so you keep \(\le\) and get \(x \le 8\). In words, x can be any value up to and including \(8\), written \(x \le 8\). The bound is closed, so \(8\) itself works. Check it, \(3(8 - 2) = 18\), and \(18 \le 18\) is true, while \(x = 9\) gives \(3(7) = 21\), which is too much. The largest value x can be is \(\boxed{8}\).

review.x: A small ferry runs a number line route where each stop is labeled by an integer position. The captain logs every stop \(x\) that is at least \(-5\) and still strictly west of \(4\), so the recorded positions satisfy \(-5 \le x < 4\). How many integer positions get logged?
hints: The left end uses \(\le\), so \(-5\) itself counts. The right end uses \(<\), so \(4\) does not count. Start your list at the smallest allowed integer and stop at the largest allowed integer.
The integers run from \(-5\) up through \(3\), since \(4\) is excluded. List them all and count, or notice that from \(-5\) to \(3\) you can subtract and adjust by one.
review.sol: The compound inequality is \(-5 \le x < 4\). No multiplying or dividing by a negative happens here, so there is no sign flip to worry about, the inequality already shows the range directly. In words, \(x\) is at least \(-5\) and also strictly less than \(4\). Because the left end is \(\le\), the value \(-5\) is included. Because the right end is \(<\), the value \(4\) is excluded, so the largest integer allowed is \(3\). The integers that satisfy it are \(-5, -4, -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3\). Counting them gives \(9\). You can also count quickly. From \(-5\) to \(3\) the gap is \(3 - (-5) = 8\), and counting both endpoints adds one more, so \(8 + 1 = 9\). The number of integer positions logged is \(\boxed{9}\).

review.x: A candle stands \(30\) centimeters tall and burns down \(2.5\) centimeters every hour it is lit. To keep sitting in its metal holder, the candle must stay at least \(5\) centimeters tall, since a shorter stub slips right out. After \(h\) hours of burning its height is \(30 - 2.5h\), so the rule for staying in the holder is \(30 - 2.5h \ge 5\). What is the MOST whole hours the candle can burn and still sit in its holder?
hints: Start from \(30 - 2.5h \ge 5\) and subtract \(30\) from both sides to get the \(h\) term alone. You should land on \(-2.5h \ge -25\).
To finish you divide both sides by \(-2.5\). Dividing by a negative flips the inequality, so \(\ge\) becomes \(\le\). Then read off the largest whole number of hours allowed.
review.sol: Begin with the condition for staying in the holder, \(30 - 2.5h \ge 5\). Subtract \(30\) from both sides to isolate the \(h\) term, which gives \(-2.5h \ge -25\). Now divide both sides by \(-2.5\). Dividing by a negative number flips the inequality, so \(\ge\) turns into \(\le\), and you get \(h \le 10\). In words, the candle stays tall enough for any burning time up to and including \(10\) hours. Check the boundary. At \(h = 10\) the height is \(30 - 2.5 \cdot 10 = 5\) centimeters, exactly the minimum, so \(10\) hours still works. At \(h = 11\) the height is \(30 - 2.5 \cdot 11 = 2.5\) centimeters, which is below \(5\), so it would slip out. The most whole hours it can burn is \(\boxed{10}\).
